2: wayyōʾmer ʾel-ʾimmô ʾelep ûmēʾāh hakkāsep ʾăšer luqqaḥ-lāk wattəʾallî wəgam ʾāmart bəʾāznāy hinnēh hakkesef ʾittî ʾănî laqāḥtîw — 'He said to his mother: The 1100 pieces of silver that were taken from you, about which you uttered a curse in my hearing — behold, the silver is with me; I took it.' ʾālāh (H422, to curse/swear/bind with an oath) — the mother had invoked a curse on the thief; Micah confesses to avoid the curse's effect. kāsep (H3701, silver/money) — 1100 pieces of silver is exactly the amount each Philistine lord paid Delilah (16:5), creating a literary echo: the narrative moves from Samson's destruction by silver to Micah's corruption by silver.
3: wayyāšeb ʾet-ʾelep ûmēʾāh hakkāsep ləʾimmô wattoʾmer ʾimmô haqḏēš hiqdaštî ʾet-hakkesef laYHWH miyyāḏî liḇnî laʿăśôt pāsael ûmassēkāh — 'He returned the 1100 pieces of silver to his mother, and his mother said: I truly dedicate the silver to YHWH from my hand for my son, to make a carved image and a molten image.' hiqḏîš (H6942, to consecrate/set apart as holy — Hiphil) — dedicating money to YHWH, then immediately directing it toward idol manufacture, is the chapter's central theological contradiction. pāsal (H6459 root, carved/graven image) and massēkāh (H4541, cast/molten image — from nāsak H5258, to pour out) — both types of idol are prohibited by the second commandment (Exod 20:4; Deut 5:8). The mother uses YHWH's name to consecrate what YHWH forbids.
5: wəhāʾîš mîkāh lô bêt ʾĕlōhîm wayyaʿaś ʾêpôḏ ûtərāpîm wayyəmallēʾ ʾet-yaḏ ʾaḥaḏ mibānāyw wayyəhî-lô ləkōhēn — 'And the man Micah had a house of gods; he made an ephod and household gods, and he installed one of his sons who became his priest.' bêt ʾĕlōhîm (house of God/gods — the same phrase as Bethel, Gen 28:17) — a private shrine mimicking the tabernacle. ʾêpôḏ (H646, ephod — the priestly garment worn by the high priest, Exod 28:4-14) — in Judges 8:27, Gideon made an ephod that 'became a snare'; here Micah makes another. tərāpîm (H8655, household gods/idols — the same objects Rachel stole from Laban, Gen 31:19; used for divination in 1 Sam 15:23; Ezek 21:21) — keeping household gods was a form of domestic religious syncretism common in ancient Canaan. millēʾ yāḏ (H4390 H3027, to fill the hand — idiom for ordaining/installing a priest, Exod 28:41; 29:9) — Micah installs his son in a priestly role, violating the Levitical monopoly on priesthood (Num 3:5-10).
6: bayyāmîm hāhēm ʾên meleḵ bəyiśrāʾēl ʾîš hayyāšār bəʿênāyw yaʿăśeh — 'In those days there was no king in Israel. Everyone did what was right in his own eyes.' The refrain appears here for the first time in its full form (cf. 18:1; 19:1; 21:25). meleḵ (H4428, king) — the absence of kingship is the structural diagnosis: without a human king, there is no covenant enforcement, no Deuteronomic accountability. hayyāšār bəʿênāyw (what is right in his own eyes) — each person substitutes personal moral judgment for covenant law. This is a reversal of the Shema: Israel should do what is right in YHWH's eyes (Deut 12:8,25,28; 13:18) — the formula here substitutes ʿênāyw (his eyes) for ʿênê YHWH (the eyes of YHWH). The theological summary indicts the entire chs17-21 appendix as the consequence of lawless self-rule.
13: wayyōʾmer mîkāh ʿattāh yāḏaʿtî kî-yêṭîḇ YHWH lî kî hāyāh lî haLēwî ləkōhēn — 'Micah said: Now I know that YHWH will do me good, because I have a Levite as priest.' Micah's theological logic is entirely superstitious: having the right ritual personnel guarantees divine blessing, regardless of what those personnel are doing. yêṭîḇ YHWH lî (YHWH will do good for me) — a prosperity assumption disconnected from covenant obedience. The Levite being hired as a private priest is itself a violation (Levites were to serve at the tabernacle under the high priest's supervision, not in private shrines). Yet Micah is satisfied because he now has a 'real' priest for his idolatrous shrine — the tragedy of religious formalism that substitutes correct procedure for correct worship.
1: wayhî-ʾîš mêhar ʾeprayim ûšəmô mîkāyəhû — 'There was a man of the hill country of Ephraim whose name was Micayahu.' mîkāyəhû (H4318, Micayahu — 'who is like YHWH?') — the name is a rhetorical question praising YHWH's incomparability (cf. Exod 15:11: mî-kāmōkā bāʾēlim YHWH). The irony is thick: a man whose name asks 'who is like YHWH?' will fill his house with idols. The hill country of Ephraim was Israelite heartland territory, not a borderland — this corruption is not a peripheral problem but central to Israel's society.
4: wayyāšeb ʾet-hakkesef ləʾimmô wayyiqqaḥ ʾimmô mēʾātayim kāsep wattitnēhû laṣṣôrep wayyaʿaśēhû pāsal ûmassēkāh wayhî bəbêt mîkāyəhû — 'He returned the silver to his mother, and his mother took 200 pieces of silver and gave them to the silversmith, who made them into a carved image and a molten image. And it was in the house of Micah.' ṣôrep (H6884, silversmith/refiner — from ṣārap, to refine metal, test by fire) — the professional craftsman executes the commission. Only 200 of the 1100 pieces go toward the idol; the rest remains with the mother. The idols are installed in the domestic space: bêt mîkāyəhû (the house of Micah) — the household shrine begins with professional-grade idols, immediately setting up the escalation in v5.
7: wayhî-naʿar mibbêt leḥem yəhûḏāh mimmišpaḥat yəhûḏāh wəhûʾ lēwî wəhûʾ gār-šam — 'Now there was a young man from Bethlehem in Judah, from the clan of Judah. He was a Levite, and he was sojourning there.' lēwî (H3878, Levite) — the Levites had no tribal territorial allotment; they were to be distributed among Israel's cities with their own towns and pasture lands (Josh 21). A Levite from Bethlehem who is 'sojourning there' — in his own tribe's territory — suggests this Levite is displaced or rootless, not settled in his designated Levitical city. This instability makes him available for hire, setting up the private chaplaincy arrangement.
8: wayyēlek hāʾîš mēhāʿîr mibbêt leḥem yəhûḏāh lāgûr baʾăšer yimṣāʾ wayyāḇōʾ har-ʾeprayim ʿaḏ-bêt mîkāh laʿăśôt darkô — 'The man departed from the city of Bethlehem in Judah to sojourn wherever he could find a place. As he traveled, he came to the hill country of Ephraim to the house of Micah.' lāgûr baʾăšer yimṣāʾ — 'to sojourn wherever he could find' — the Levite is economically migrating: searching for a place of residence and provision. The Levitical system required Israel to support the Levites (Deut 14:29; 18:1-8), but in the period of 'no king in Israel,' that system has broken down. The Levite arrives at Micah's house by apparently random travel (darkô, 'his road/way') — but the narrative will show YHWH's judgment working through this encounter.
9: wayyōʾmer lô mîkāh mēʾayin tāḇôʾ wayyōʾmer ʾēlāyw lēwî ʾānôkî mibbêt leḥem yəhûḏāh wəʾānōkî hōlēk lāgûr baʾăšer ʾimṣāʾ — 'Micah said to him: Where do you come from? He said to him: I am a Levite from Bethlehem in Judah, and I am going to sojourn wherever I may find a place.' The Levite's self-identification as a Levite (lēwî ʾānôkî) immediately interests Micah: he needs a legitimate priest for his shrine, and a genuine Levite is better than his son (v5). The exchange is transactional from the start: Micah asks 'where from?' — a standard hospitality question — and the Levite's answer triggers Micah's opportunistic offer in v10.
10: wayyōʾmer lô mîkāh šəḇāh ʿimmādî wahyēh lî ləʾāḇ ûləkōhēn wəʾānōkî ʾettēn-ləkā ʿăśeret kəsep layyāmîm ûʿerek bəḡāḏîm ûmiḥyātekā — 'Micah said to him: Stay with me, and be to me a father and a priest, and I will give you ten pieces of silver a year and a suit of clothes and your food.' ləʾāḇ ûləkōhēn (to be a father and a priest) — 'father' here is a term of honor and authority: a spiritual advisor and authority figure in the household. The employment contract is explicit: ten silver pieces annually, one set of clothing, and food — a standard service arrangement. The Levite is hired as a domestic chaplain for an idolatrous shrine: his priestly legitimacy is being rented to cover an illegitimate religious enterprise.
11: wayyôʾel halēwî lāšeḇet ʾet-hāʾîš wayhî hannáʿar lô kəʾaḥaḏ mibbānāyw — 'And the Levite agreed to stay with the man, and the young man was to him like one of his sons.' wayyôʾel (H2974, to be willing/consent/agree) — the Levite consents to the arrangement. kəʾaḥaḏ mibbānāyw (like one of his sons) — Micah adopts the young Levite into quasi-filial status: the previous family-priest son (v5) is replaced by a professional. The family relationship language masks a contractual arrangement: the Levite is an employee, not a family member, despite the domestic metaphor. This pseudo-adoption language will collapse when a better offer comes (ch18).
12: wayyəmallēʾ mîkāh ʾet-yaḏ halēwî wayhî-lô hannáʿar ləkōhēn wayhî bəbêt mîkāh — 'And Micah filled the hand of the Levite, and the young man became his priest, and was in the house of Micah.' millēʾ yāḏ (to fill the hand — the ordination formula, Exod 28:41; 29:9; Lev 8:33) — Micah formally installs the Levite using proper ordination language for an entirely improper purpose. The ordination formula belongs to the tabernacle consecration of Aaron and his sons under divine command; Micah applies it to a private shrine with hired personnel. The gap between correct ritual language and corrupt religious practice is the chapter's defining irony.
